title: Defining Methods 🛠️
description: Learn to write reusable blocks of code called Methods.

slides.title: Dry Code 🌵
slides.content:
A huge rule in programming is D.R.Y: **Don't Repeat Yourself!**

If you find yourself copying and pasting the exact same 10 lines of code in multiple places, you are breaking the DRY rule.

Instead, you should bundle those 10 lines into a **Method**.

slides.title: What is a Method?
slides.content:
A Method is a named block of code. Think of it like a custom command you are teaching Ruby to execute.

To define a method, you use the `def` keyword, followed by the name you want to give it, and closed with an `end`.

```ruby
def say_hello
  puts "Hello there!"
end
```

slides.title: Calling a Method
slides.content:
Just defining a method doesn't run it (you are just teaching Ruby the command).

To actually run the code inside, you must *Call* it by typing its name.
slides.codeSnippet:
# 1. Defining the method
def cheer
  puts "Hooray!"
end

# 2. Calling the method
cheer
cheer
cheer

quizData.question: Which keyword is used to DEFINE a brand new method?
quizData.explanation: `def` stands for 'define'. Once a method is defined using `def...end`, you can call on it anytime.

slides.title: Method Naming Rules 📏
slides.content:
Just like variables, method names should describe what they do and follow the `snake_case` convention.

Good: `calculate_tax`
Bad: `CalculateTax`

Also, a method name should generally be a verb, because methods *do actions*.

quizData.question: What happens if you define a method but never actually call it?

options.text: The method runs exactly once by default.
The code inside the method is entirely ignored by Ruby.

quizData.explanation: Defining a method just stores those instructions in memory forever. They will literally never execute unless you ask Ruby to run them by "calling" the method name.
